title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several contracts and resolutions, including awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, recommending approval of a state transportation funding resolution, and establishing a $100/day fine for sanitary sewer discharge violations. The committee also learned the village secured a local road improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Recycled asphalt base course production contract
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkey Avenue duplex

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a local road improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, a project estimated at $1.5 million with over 50% grant funding. The project will likely be moved from 2029 to 2028. Michael noted the village has secured over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue road repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for settling issues over sewer trenches, with the committee emphasizing the contractor must return if problems recur. A special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue was scheduled for Thursday, May 28 at 6:00 PM.

Staff reported spring activities including road limit removal, mailbox and sign repairs, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Significant dewatering was required near Weston Avenue due to flooding, with discussion about potential permanent pump solutions. Two culverts failed on Jones Street requiring replacement. Equipment maintenance and changeover from winter to summer operations was detailed. The committee discussed the need for better water management solutions in the Zenzer/Shore Avenue area.

Josh reported on water service repairs, well maintenance, and significant clear water infiltration issues in the sanitary sewer system. In April, 37 million gallons of water entered the distribution system but 53-54 million gallons were treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. The Kathleen lift station showed clear evidence of sump pump discharge into sanitary sewers. An INI study with in-pipe technologies will help identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with prior year. Water loss was 6%. Utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility has approximately $6.8 million in cash reserves and serves about 6,100 customers total. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions at the state level. He noted grant funding helps one-time costs but recurring operational funding remains inadequate, requiring general tax subsidies. The committee voted unanimously to recommend village board adoption of Resolution 2026-014.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Analysis showed this saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to purchasing commercially, resulting in about $130,000 savings across 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on fines for illegal sump pump and clear water discharges to the sanitary system. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. The committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. After discussion, the committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100 per day for first offenses. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael explained a builder wants to construct a duplex on a lot without direct main frontage. Staff proposed allowing a temporary long lateral connection until Radkey Avenue reconstruction extends mains. The committee added language requiring reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion to recommend approval with the 2029 deadline passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed an intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction. Weston's share for water main relocation is estimated at $94,860. The water main must be relocated due to storm sewer conflicts. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For the Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project, MSA bid $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue, MSA bid $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730. Staff recommended MSA for the roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel due to having full-time experienced staff versus an intern. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Award recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with $100/day fine
Recommend approval of temporary water/sewer connection at 2230 Radkey Avenue with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Recommend approval of Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild ($94,860 village share)
Award construction services: MSA for Alderson/Jelinek roundabout, Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Send notices about water main flushing starting next week
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs Wednesday/Thursday

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting following reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Work product transmittals, educational presentations and reports
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated for chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated for vice chair by a commissioner, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and approved as secretary. There was brief procedural confusion about whether the vice chair nomination had been seconded.
Minutes from April 21st were approved unanimously with a motion and second by Renee.

discussions.item: Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) represented the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield hot air balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making their event the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members questioned room usage, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request for better hotel room tracking data in post-event reports to help justify future funding decisions. Motion passed unanimously.

Renee reported attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, including winner Anastasia, and noted the candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese. This was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow Balloonfest to provide hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously with the commission addressing concerns about building orientation due to a gas pipeline easement and establishing twice-yearly inspections for salt storage facilities near municipal wells.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive (taken out of order as Item 6)
Unfinished business - Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline easement running through the property, which reduced fill costs significantly. The parking lot will be constructed over the 36-inch deep gas line, which is acceptable to the gas company with an observer present during construction. The building has capacity for 50-60 officers (current staffing is 40 officers and 4 clerical). An alternate storage shed is proposed as a bid alternate depending on costs. Discussion addressed future expansion possibilities to the north and south, and optimal placement of the alternate shed. Motion to approve was made and passed unanimously subject to staff conditions 2-5.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff proposed a new inspection program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg that predates the wellhead protection ordinance and has never gone through the conditional use process. Commission members agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Motion to approve the program passed unanimously, with an amendment to report back to both the Plan Commission and Public Works Committee.
Motion to approve as presented passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
discussions.body: Approved unanimously with no discussion.
Approved unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Staff announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send email notification and calendar invite to commissioners.

publicComment: Jim Pensel (5002 Arrow Street, Weston) spoke via online connection. He requested the commission use common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project to avoid excessive taxpayer costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building. He also asked that the commission request annual reports on the salt shed inspection program to ensure proper oversight of facilities in wellhead protection zones.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved and recommended to Board of Trustees
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection districts with twice-yearly inspections
Staff to report back to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee on inspection program results in approximately one year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that predates wellhead protection ordinance to bring into compliance
Joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to share Ehlers presentation on residential TIF districts with commission
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in future meeting (fall timeframe)

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from the Health Department, Veteran Services, and Aging Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and received educational briefings on department missions, budgets, and services with no other action items.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote attendance confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute discussion
Review of standing committee ordinance 2.04 including missions and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Discussion of health department budget and organizational structure
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Department presentation by VSO Erin Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting in favor.

discussions.item: Review of standing committee ordinance and structure
discussions.body: Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction over Veterans Service Department, Social Services Department, Health Department, North Central Health Care, and ADRC of Central Wisconsin. He outlined reporting relationships with various boards including the Social Services Board, Veterans Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the committee will be working on a new strategic plan expected by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's 37.6 FTE staff across four divisions: environmental health, nursing, community health improvement, and administration. She highlighted over 1,200 licenses overseen, a new homeless system response position, Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates, public health vending machines with free resources including tick kits, and lead mitigation efforts. Administrator Leonard noted approximately half of the $5 million budget is tax levy with significant intergovernmental and fee revenues.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa, noting the department has nearly 140 FTE and a $22 million budget with approximately $7 million in tax levy. He highlighted the Family Keys housing stability pilot program partnership with the state involving Marathon, LaCrosse, and Wood counties, which has been very successful. He indicated future restructuring requests may come as the department addresses increased needs in child support and other units.

VSO Erin Galindo reported his department increased to 3.0 FTE in 2025 and had 8,149 client interactions, up from 5,900 the previous year. He noted $44 million in annual VA benefits flow to Marathon County veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo highlighted partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues for housing, suicide prevention initiatives (noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans), and work on surviving spouse benefits. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have a VSO but offices collaborate well across county lines.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood) with about 75 staff and 385-400 volunteers. He noted 11% of budget is county levy with the remainder being state and federal funding. Ray highlighted significant challenges with the nutrition program as food costs increased 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat, forcing meal and staffing reductions. Marathon County's 2026 contribution is $413,135. Vice Chair Rosenberg thanked the program for culturally appropriate meals at the Hmong American Center.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
Mike Ray to present ADRC annual report with 2025 data to full board
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion items to future meetings
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as initially stated)

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, and e-bikes
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment criticizing the lack of detailed cost data for park maintenance. Trustee Motti expressed support for the fees given population growth. Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously, as was Resolution 2026-015 amending the fee schedule.

Chief Finkey explained this ordinance establishes a reciprocal inspection agreement between Safer and other municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and Cloverland. A food truck inspected by one participating department receives a sticker recognized by all others, eliminating redundant inspections. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the policy. Trustees Beck and others argued the policy should match what was previously voted on - removing specific justification requirements for remote attendance. An amendment was made to strike sentences requiring health, safety, or welfare justifications, keeping only the requirements to notify 12 hours in advance, be on camera, and no participation in closed sessions. The amendment passed with two nos from Trustees Hermling and Motti. The original motion as amended passed with the same two dissenting votes.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopez Sorrell, passed unanimously. The president noted openings remain on Parks, Finance/HR, and one more on CLIPS. Board agreed to include applicant resumes in future packets.

discussions.item: Secondhand article dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC
discussions.body: Chief Hunt explained the license requirement under Wisconsin statute 134.71 for dealers buying and selling precious metals. The board amended the motion to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit since the building at 5703 Jamar Street is still under construction. Both the amendment and original motion as amended passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Farmers market location at Kennedy Park discussion
discussions.body: This was discussion only with no action taken. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about parking on the grass along Alderson. Trustee Motti noted the previous Camp Phillips/Scoffield location was donated by Lockery and is now being developed. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots since moving. Mont Miller Park was discussed but rejected due to poor visibility. Trustee Beck questioned costs, but Renee confirmed the market operates at no loss through vendor fees and grants for programs like double up food bucks.

Director of Public Works Michael Dolan reported a speed study showed the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph, though the engineering study on file recommended 35 mph. He needs to conduct additional data collection after construction is complete. A resident noted the current speed is inconsistent with the ordinance and this will need to come back to the board.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000, questioning why the engineering technician position remains unfilled, and complaining about Lisa Beck being introduced as a Safer board member before official appointment. He also criticized the lack of detailed Kennedy Park information in the packet and called for more administrator accountability.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased effective upon ordinance passage
Engineering technician position continues to be advertised
Speed limit study for Weston Avenue to be completed after construction and brought back to board
Intergovernmental service agreement to be drafted for building inspection assistance to Town of Ringle
Comprehensive Plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 pm
Committee and commission applicant resumes to be included in future board packets
Mobile food vendor reciprocal inspection program implemented
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that impacted Ringle and other communities. The board suspended its rules to act quickly on the tipping fee relief measure.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Chair remarks on committee appointments and leadership responsibilities
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster relief

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs outlined goals for committee appointments including increasing diversity of perspectives, ensuring leadership development for newer supervisors (34% in first or second term), and honoring supervisor preferences. Supervisor Cavelli moved and Supervisor Crims seconded approval of all committee appointments including county administrator appointments. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg, who praised her advocacy for children and elders), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Paper ballots were distributed and counted. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. No discussion occurred. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. No discussion occurred. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill Tipping Fee Modification for Disaster Relief
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher explained the proposal to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that caused significant damage in Ringle and other communities. The state waived its $13.10 fee, Ringle waived its $2.33 fee, and the county proposed reducing fees to approximately $36.94 per ton (covering costs only) for certified disaster debris. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely for affected residents. Multiple supervisors asked questions about implementation, timeline, and future policy. The board unanimously suspended rules to consider the resolution, then unanimously approved Resolution 26-26. Administrator Leonard indicated ongoing discussions with the Community Foundation about additional relief options.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special permission to speak during the tipping fee discussion. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County waive fees entirely for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle had all waived their fees and asking for county contribution to help affected residents. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of homeowners facing demolition, noting many insurance adjusters had not yet arrived and requesting more time and fee relief for underinsured residents.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation regarding reimbursement program for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board directed to develop clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Staff to track all disaster-related waste using certification forms and monitor cleanup progress for potential extension beyond 60 days

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and passed an amended resolution to waive only reconnect fees (not all fees) for properties impacted by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing - Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing - Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation - Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation - Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation - Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for property owners James Offer and David Tract. The Town of Green Valley Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from conservancy recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature, and Pogle explained the property currently has a home and horse riding stable, with the rezone making the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve and forward to county board, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for tornado-affected properties in Ringle, Kronenwetter, Easton, and Weston areas. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Leur asked about tracking costs. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about fairness to those impacted by smaller disasters not declared emergencies, and questioned giving fee breaks when fees were recently raised. Drebeck proposed an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees ($160 each) for pouts systems, excluding other zoning or septic fees. The amendment passed, and the amended resolution was approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director David Hagenbusher presented an overview of solid waste operations, including the landfill's 45-year history, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy that began injecting gas in September 2025, and upcoming challenges including leachate treatment requirements by 2032 due to PFAS concerns. He noted significant capital expenses including $6 million for 11-acre expansion and $3-4 million closures. Marilyn Bend praised the department for keeping costs competitive.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department educational presentation
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie. The department operates 17 county parks, 30,700 acres of county forest across 10 units, and provides services to the City of Wausau. A Nine Mile County Forest study showed out-of-town visitors generate $3.25 million for the county economy. Supervisor Sefeld announced that Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service, with Joe Tucker selected as the new county forest administrator.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. No one signed up for public comment at the beginning of the meeting, and no public testimony was offered during either rezoning public hearing.
actionItems: Rezoning petition for James Offer and David Tract (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) approved and forwarded to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Rezoning petition for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) approved and forwarded to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Amended resolution to waive only pouts reconnect fees ($160) for April 17, 2026 tornado-impacted properties forwarded to county board for consideration
Town of Green Valley recommended to update comprehensive plan to reflect zoning change if applicable
Town of McMillan recommended to update comprehensive plan to reflect zoning change if applicable

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center due to the chair's absence, approved installation of kayak launch signs along the Plover River, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising efforts and the Yellow Banks disc golf course. Public commenters expressed opposition to dogs at the pool and questioned Kennedy Park fundraising transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Oaklair River Trail discussion

discussions.body: Minutes from the previous meeting were approved with a motion and second. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Director Sean Oster provided an update including a timeline document prepared with Michael showing the project's history from 2023 to present. Scott asked about village capital dollars for Kennedy Park; Sean confirmed no village funds are currently earmarked as those items were pulled from capital improvement plans pending the project. The Friends of Kennedy Park are currently fundraising for lights on the upper field, needing $117,000 with approximately $85,000 already at the community foundation. Sean noted the friends group plans a public check presentation at their June tournament. Committee members emphasized this is a long-term 10-year plan and defended the volunteer friends group against public criticism about transparency.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Sean presented photos and a PDF showing the location of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during construction. Dan from the disc golf association showed photos of the mat installation process. The mats are owned by the village with some extras available for other trail projects.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the next meeting because Katrina, who requested the item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. An audience member named Andy (6504 [street name inaudible]) had attended specifically to oppose the idea, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' His objection was noted and he indicated he may return next month.

Sean explained challenges with graffiti removal, noting the pressure washer cannot access the trail due to bridge weight limits and steep ditches. Staff has tried various graffiti removers with limited success. Committee members suggested anti-vandalism coatings or sealers, and the possibility of volunteer workdays for maintenance. Sean said they would investigate cleaning options but noted access limitations require frozen conditions for heavy equipment. No formal action was taken.

Dan from the kayak association presented version 7 of informational signs for kayak launches along the Plover River. Three of eight launches are in the Village of Weston (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). Signs will feature maps, QR codes for river gauge information, and location-specific history. Marathon County Parks Foundation is funding the signs through state grants. The committee approved a motion for the village to install the signs, to be forwarded to the village board. Motion carried unanimously.

The committee will bring back the dog day at aquatic center discussion. Sean raised a request from a community member about placing food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds. Committee agreed to add this for future discussion, though members expressed mixed feelings about setting precedent for other groups making similar requests.

Sean reported the aquatic center pool repairs were completed, painting of dugouts is complete, and the pool is being filled (about 3 feet shy of full). Pool opening day is June 6th with training this weekend and rentals starting next Wednesday. Sean also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler next Tuesday, and noted the final bike and pedestrian plan open house is June 11th from 5-7pm.

publicComment: Two members of the public commented. Andy (6504 [street name]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating animals should not be allowed in public pools and belong in rivers and lakes. Jim Pensan (5002 [street name], appearing online under 'Vern M') criticized the 4:00 pm meeting time as discouraging public participation, and questioned Kennedy Park funding transparency, calling the $8,700 expenditure figure 'too coincidental' and asking where grant money and glow event proceeds went, suggesting the Friends of Kennedy Park are 'frauds' for not sharing complete financials.
actionItems: Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate graffiti cleaning options and anti-vandalism coatings for Oaklair River Trail
Motion approved to install kayak launch signs at village landings - forwarded to village board
Food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds added to future agenda
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler next Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved minutes, acknowledged budget reports, and received an educational presentation on the Municipal Clerk's Office operations. Committee member Evan Erdling announced his resignation from the committee due to moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
TIF one and TIF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's Office budget and operations
Staff remarks and farmers market discussion

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

The committee reviewed the April budget status report, TIF one and TIF two detail reports, and legal details report. Regarding TIF closure, Jessica explained the bonds are due October 1st and the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, likely this summer. She confirmed the village will be able to pay off the debt. Motion to acknowledge items 5, 6, and 7 was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper, passing unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented an overview of clerk office operations, staffing (herself and Sarah full-time, Leah part-time), and budgets. Key points included: elections budget varies significantly between even years (presidential/gubernatorial) and odd years; postage jumped from $6,000 to $15,000 due to fall elections; Badger Books cost about $11,000 for four units; pet licensing was brought back in-house saving $5,495 annually from the previous Pet Data contract; and the quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for printing and postage. Discussion occurred about potential cost savings on the newsletter, with staff noting previous attempts to eliminate paper copies were met with resident complaints requesting their return.

Pam presented survey data from 52 Marathon County municipalities showing Weston's election inspector pay of $12/hour is average, but chief inspector pay is on the lower end. She indicated she would likely propose increasing chief inspector pay to $15/hour when the two-year term comes around due to the significant responsibilities involved.

Discussion addressed why pet licensing fees doubled from $10 to $20. Pam explained Marathon County raised fees charged to municipalities because they were only collecting $65,000 while paying $100,000 annually to the Humane Society. The fee increase was passed to municipalities. A non-dog stray contract with Mountain Bay Metro was discussed, with one committee member noting that eight mice cost $175 each to process, suggesting future policy review.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park is going well, with full vendor slots including food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and greenhouse vendors. Some vendors have had to leave on windy Tuesdays. Produce vendors expected to increase mid to late June. Committee members discussed parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff clarifying parking is now allowed on the west side of Alderson only.

Evan Erdling announced this was his last meeting as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though remaining in the metro area. He thanked the committee for the opportunity to serve. The chair noted there are now open spots on this and other committees and encouraged members to reach out to interested citizens.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Minutes from previous meeting approved
April budget status report, TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports, and legal details report acknowledged
CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIF bonds due October 1st
Committee has open positions requiring citizen recruitment
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Public Works Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, this meeting was scheduled to address a public hearing on special assessments for the Alderson St and Jelinek Ave intersection project, along with multiple infrastructure resolutions and project updates. The meeting was significant as it involved property owner assessments for driveway approach construction related to a new roundabout installation.

agenda.item: Alderson St and Jelinek Ave Intersection Project Special Assessments Public Hearing
Approval of 5/11/26 Public Works & Utility Committee Minutes
Approval of 5/28/26 Bloedel Ave Special Assessment Hearing Minutes

discussions.item: Alderson St and Jelinek Ave Intersection Project Special Assessments Public Hearing
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to conduct a public hearing on special assessments for driveway approach construction related to a project replacing the existing 4-way stop intersection with a compact roundabout. Property owners were expected to have opportunity for testimony and questions, with assessments calculated at $8.61 per square foot for concrete driveway aprons and prorated for driveways installed within the past 20 years.

The committee was set to consider approval of the 2025 Compliance Maintenance Annual Report for Weston Sewer Utility for submission to the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources. This is a required annual regulatory compliance report.

The committee was expected to consider an authorizing resolution for the reconstruction of Bloedel Ave from Pine Park St to Alderson St. This follows a special assessment hearing held on May 28, 2026.

discussions.item: Resolution 2026-018 – Alderson St and Jelinek Ave Intersection Project
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to consider authorization for the Alderson St and Jelinek Ave intersection project, which involves replacing the 4-way stop with a roundabout, along with water main relocation, sanitary sewer improvements, and storm sewer replacement.
The committee was set to review quotes for topsoil screening services. The agenda did not provide details on the scope or vendors submitting quotes.
The committee was scheduled to review paving quotes for Shirley Ave. Specific details on project scope were not included in the agenda.

discussions.item: Contract Amendment No. 1 with MSA for Right of Way Services
discussions.body: The committee was expected to consider a contract amendment with MSA for right of way services related to the Alderson St and Jelinek Ave intersection project.

discussions.item: Change Order No. 1 for Alderson St and Jelinek Ave Project
discussions.body: The committee was set to review the first change order for the Alderson St and Jelinek Ave intersection project. Details on the nature of the change order were not specified in the agenda.

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to nuisance ordinances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, and authorized demolition of an abandoned structure. The committee also discussed changes to overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request from Mount Olive Church, deferring both items for further information.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Department reports - Code enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: Code enforcement officer reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the enforcement list after the property sale was completed and the new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week and the 39th starting in May. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 was made by Sherwanka, seconded by Olsen, and passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including the tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet) that were switched in the text as discussed in previous meetings. Motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees was made by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, and passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion occurred regarding emotos (electric dirt bikes). Chief Hunt strongly advocated for prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, and trails, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee members discussed whether to also restrict electric scooters but decided to leave officer discretion for kids' play scooters. Rothschild has not yet approved the model ordinance. Motion by Olsen to recommend approval with the addition that emotos are prohibited from operating on roadways, sidewalks, trails, or any public property was seconded by Sherwanka and passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4 AM-4 PM) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities which typically have 5-hour windows. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plows arrive. Public Works Director Michael suggested identifying 6-8 problem areas for specific signage instead of village-wide restrictions. The committee deferred action, directing Michael to identify specific exception areas to bring back in July.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their church and DC Everest High School parking lot where parishioners park on Sundays. Committee members expressed reluctance to pay for the crosswalk, estimating $2,000-3,000 for a basic crosswalk or $25,000-30,000 for a lighted system. Motion by Olsen that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the installation cost, the committee recommends board approval was seconded by Sherwanka and passed unanimously. Michael will contact Mount Olive regarding cost-sharing.

Fire Chief explained a new regional inspection program where multiple municipalities (Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston) will recognize each other's food truck fire inspections. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Fire Marshal Marty recently inspected a truck that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane cylinders. Motion by Sherwanka to approve Ordinance 26-8 was seconded by Campkey and passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing second address), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkkey Avenue near a new duplex development. Chief Hunt noted an additional issue with Weston Villa Apartments having nine separate addresses for one eight-unit structure. Motion to recommend board approval was made and seconded by Campkey, passing unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin demolition proceedings for an abandoned structure that has been vacant since 2010-2011. A previous fire department training burn was cancelled due to unsafe conditions. The current owners, who are up to date on taxes, previously housed chickens in the structure. Demolition costs will be placed on the tax roll for recovery. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding multiple agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at the park, predicting the grass will be destroyed by summer's end. He noted that parking vehicles on grass appears to violate village code and questioned the placement of ADA parking spaces on grass rather than asphalt. He questioned the origin of the new food truck fire inspection requirement. He also expressed concern about long-standing code violations on the enforcement list, noting some cases are very old with no visible progress.
actionItems: Chapter 50 nuisance amendments recommended to board of trustees for approval
E-bicycle ordinance with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property recommended to board
Public Works Director to identify 6-8 specific areas needing parking restrictions for July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding willingness to pay for crosswalk installation
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved
Address corrections for Delicoski Street, Ross Avenue, and Radkkey Avenue recommended to board
Raze order authorized for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property condition at Sternberg and South Timber intersection
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 PM

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
